content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

December 4, 2017

Eric Lucan
Councilmember, City of Novato
26 Truman Drive
Novato, CA 94947

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 17-267

Dear Mr. Lucan:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. We are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

Does the Act prohibit your participation in decisions regarding the Atherton Place Project ("Project") where you own a duplex rental property 1700 feet from the Project?

CONCLUSION

No. Based upon the facts provided, you do not have a conflict of interest because there is no indication that the Project will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on your interests.

FACTS

The Atherton Place Project involves the development of 50 townhomes and 1,360 square-feet of retail space on a vacant 3.60-acre site. The project consists of twelve blocks and proposed buildings will be two or three-stories (up to 38-feet high). The townhomes will be sold to individual

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-267
Page No. 2

owners and the retail space will be leased. A total of 145 parking spaces are provided onsite and 19
on-street parking spaces are proposed. The applicant is proposing to pay a fee in-lieu of providing
affordable units in Atherton Place. Atherton Place is subject to state subdivision laws and a prior
version of Novato's Affordable Housing Ordinance that exempts the project from constructing
affordable units.

This Project was originally submitted in 2007, and stalled due to a controversial update of
the General Plan and Housing Element update. The Novato City Council is considering a Master
Plan Amendment, Precise Development Plan Amendment, Vesting Tentative Map, and Design
Review. Some modifications to the Project, such as increased parking and less intensity of use, have
occurred in the past two years. The Master Plan Amendment modifies the previously approved
retail and office development to reflect current uses and intensity for the Project. The Precise
Development Plan Amendment would replace language addressing the retail/office development
previously approved on the Project Site with text reflecting the specific design of the Project,
including building coverage, setbacks, height limit, parking requirements, accessory use and
structure limitations, and other relevant site planning and development standard requirements. The
Vesting Tentative Subdivision Map decision would merge the two existing parcels comprising the
Project site into a single common interest parcel and create a single commercial condominium
space and air space rights for the condominimium units. The Design Review decisions relates to the
Project's site design options, building height/massing, and landscape and architectural concepts.

You own a duplex rental property near Highway 101, and located five "large city blocks"
from the proposed Project. The property is diagonally separated by a significant roadway, Redwood
Boulevard, or "Old Highway 101." Your rental property is immediately adjacent to the historic
downtown, which is predominantly built out. This minimizes impacts on intensity of use. You state
that the project does not appear to present changes to the character of the rental property, in that you
do not anticipate substantial changes in items such as traffic levels, parking, view, privacy, noise, or
the air quality in the area.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in the
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a
"financial interest" in a decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally, on one or more of the official's interests identified in that section. Applicable to the facts
provided, Section 87103 identifies the following interests:

• Any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment
worth $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)), or in which the official is a director,
officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section
87103(d)).

• Any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).)

File No. A-17-267
Page No. 3

• Any source of income aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the official within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).)

You have identified a business interest in your rental business, a real property interest in your rental duplex, and a source of income interest in your tenants.

Foreseeability and Materiality

A decision's financial effect on an official's interest is presumed reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, the decision. (Regulation 18701(a).) An interest is the "subject" of a proceeding if the decision involves the license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the interest, including a decision affecting real property under Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6). Your facts do not implicate any of the factors in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6), and your property is not the subject of the Project decisions.

Where the interest is not a named party in or the subject of the decision, the decision's financial effect is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701(b).)

As to materiality, we look to Regulation 18702.1, the materiality standard for a business entity.² Regulation 18702.1(b) is applicable to these facts, and provides that the financial effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find that the decision would contribute to a change in the business' value. Examples include decisions that would:

(1) Authorize, prohibit, regulate or otherwise establish conditions for an activity in which the business entity is engaged;

(2) Increase or decrease the amount of competition in the field in which the business entity is engaged;

(3) Increase or decrease the need for the products or services that the business entity supplies;

(4) Make improvements in the surrounding neighborhood such as redevelopment projects, traffic/road improvements, or parking changes that may affect, either temporarily or permanently, the amount of business the business entity receives;

(5) Decide the location of a major development, entertainment facility, or other project that would increase or decrease the amount of business the entity draws from the location of the project; or

___________________________
² For an interest in real property containing a business entity, Regulation 18702.2(b)(8) states that the materiality standards for a business entity in Regulation 18702.1 apply if the decision may change the income producing potential of the property and the nature of the business entity remains unchanged. Moreover, there is no indication that the decision would affect the property under other provisions in Regulation 18702.2 such as a change in the development potential of the parcel, the highest or best use of the parcel, or the character of the real property.

File No. A-17-267
Page No. 4

(6) Increase or decrease the tax burden, debt, or financial or legal liability of the
business entity.

None of the above factors are implicated for your rental property. The Project relates to
town homes that will be sold to individual owners, not rental units, and it is unlikely that the units
will increase or decrease the rental property market. Additionally, your property is not in the
surrounding neighborhood and is separated from the area of the Project by a major roadway. None
of the facts indicate that the Project will foreseeably and materially affect your rental business.³

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Assistant General Counsel

By: L. Karen Harrison
Counsel, Legal Division

LKH:jgl

³ In regard to your interest in your tenants as sources of income, an effect on a source of income is material
only if the individual will receive a measurable financial benefit or loss or you know or have reason to know that the
person has a business interest or real property materially affected by the decision. (Regulations 18702.3 and 18702.5.)
In this case, there is no indication the tenants will receive a financial benefit or loss and no foreseeable material effect
on the leased property. Provided you do not know or have reason to know a tenant has any other business entity or
property affected by the decision, your interest in the tenant as a source of income does not disqualify you from the
decisions.

QUESTION: Does the Act prohibit your participation in decisions regarding the Atherton Place Project 
("Project") where you own a duplex rental property 1700 feet from the Project?

CONCLUSION: No. Based upon the facts provided, you do not have a conflict of interest because there is no 
indication that the Project will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on your 
interests.
